object_oriented_programming: Classes - A blueprint or template for creating objects with properties and methods
Objects - Instances of classes that contain specific data and can perform actions
Inheritance - Creating new classes based on existing ones to reuse code and extend functionality
Polymorphism - Using the same interface (method name) for different implementations across different classes
Encapsulation - Hiding internal implementation details and exposing only what's necessary through public methods
Abstraction - Simplifying complex real-world problems by modeling only essential features
data_structures: Arrays/Lists - Ordered collections of elements accessed by numeric index, useful for storing multiple items
Linked Lists - Collections where each element points to the next, allowing dynamic growth but slower access
Hash Maps/Dictionaries - Key-value pairs with fast O(1) lookup time, ideal for associating values with unique keys
Sets - Unordered collections of unique elements, useful for membership testing and removing duplicates
Trees - Hierarchical data structures with parent-child relationships, used in file systems and databases
Graphs - Collections of nodes connected by edges, modeling relationships like social networks
algorithms: Sorting algorithms - Methods to arrange data in order: bubble sort, merge sort, quick sort, each with different complexity
Searching algorithms - Finding elements in data: linear search O(n), binary search O(log n) for sorted data
Recursion - Function calling itself with a base case, useful for tree structures and divide-and-conquer
Dynamic Programming - Breaking problems into overlapping subproblems and caching results for efficiency
Greedy algorithms - Making locally optimal choices at each step, useful for optimization problems
functional_programming: Pure Functions - Functions that always return the same output for same input, with no side effects
Immutability - Data that cannot be changed after creation, making code more predictable
Higher-Order Functions - Functions that take other functions as arguments or return functions
Closures - Functions that capture and remember variables from their parent scope
Functional Composition - Combining simple functions to create more complex operations
web_development: Client-Server Architecture - Separation between frontend (client) and backend (server) handling different responsibilities
REST API - Architectural style using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for CRUD operations
HTTP Methods - GET (retrieve), POST (create), PUT (update), DELETE (remove) for web operations
Authentication - Verifying user identity using passwords, tokens, or other mechanisms
Caching - Storing frequently accessed data to improve performance and reduce server load
design_principles: DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) - Write code once and reuse it, avoiding duplication and maintenance issues
SOLID Principles - Five design principles for maintainable code: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov, Interface, Dependency
KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) - Prefer simple solutions over complex ones unless complexity is necessary
YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) - Don't add features you don't currently need, avoid over-engineering
